questions.q: Which communication protocol requires only two wires (SDA and SCL) and supports multiple slave devices on a shared bus?
options: UART
SPI
I2C
CAN bus
questions.explanation: I2C (Inter-Integrated Circuit) uses two wires (data and clock) with open-drain outputs and pull-up resistors, allowing multiple slave devices to share the bus. UART uses separate TX/RX lines, SPI requires four lines including separate chip selects, and CAN uses differential signaling on a single bus pair.

questions.q: What is a key advantage of SPI (Serial Peripheral Interface) over I2C?
options: SPI uses fewer pins for communication
SPI offers much higher data transfer rates due to independent clock and no arbitration overhead
SPI supports more slave devices on a single bus
SPI requires less power consumption
questions.explanation: SPI achieves higher speeds (often MHz range, vs I2C's kHz range) because it uses a dedicated clock line and separate slave select signals without bus arbitration. Trade-offs include requiring more pins (CS lines per device) and limited distance. Both can support multiple devices, but SPI is inherently faster.

questions.q: In UART communication, what does the baud rate represent?
options: The total number of bits transmitted per communication session
The number of bits transmitted per second
The voltage level of the transmitted signal
The physical distance the signal can travel
questions.explanation: Baud rate is the number of signal changes per second; in UART, it typically equals the bit rate (bits per second) since each baud represents one bit. Common rates are 9600, 115200 bps. Baud rate must match between sender and receiver for correct interpretation.

questions.q: What is the primary purpose of the CAN (Controller Area Network) bus in automotive and industrial applications?
options: To provide high-speed data transfer between distant computer systems
To enable multiple embedded devices to communicate on a single twisted-pair bus with built-in error detection and collision resolution
To replace Wi-Fi in embedded systems
To provide real-time display output to monitors
questions.explanation: CAN bus allows numerous microcontrollers to share a single communication line with message filtering, error detection (CRC), and collision resolution (CSMA/CA). Originally designed for vehicles, it's now used industrially. It's robust for harsh environments but slower than Ethernet.

questions.q: In I2C protocol, what is the significance of open-drain outputs and pull-up resistors?
options: They increase the signal voltage to dangerous levels
They allow multiple devices to share the bus safely by letting any device pull the line low, with resistors returning it high when no device is pulling
They provide power to slave devices
They amplify weak signals
questions.explanation: I2C's open-drain architecture means devices can only actively pull lines low; resistors passively pull lines high when released. This allows multiple devices to safely share the bus without contention—a critical feature for multi-master or multi-slave configurations. This is why I2C is called a 'wired-AND' bus.

questions.q: Which protocol is best suited for point-to-point communication between two microcontrollers with minimal wiring?
options: SPI (requiring 4+ wires)
CAN bus (requiring twisted pair)
UART (requiring only TX and RX lines)
I2C (requiring pull-up resistors)
questions.explanation: UART is ideal for simple point-to-point links with just two wires (transmit and receive). No clock synchronization or addressing is needed for two devices. The other protocols are designed for multi-device scenarios or longer distances, adding unnecessary complexity for simple point-to-point connections.

questions.q: What is the main trade-off between I2C's low pin count and its performance characteristics?
options: I2C is faster but requires more pins
I2C uses fewer pins but operates at lower speeds (typically 100-400 kHz) due to bus arbitration and capacitive loading
I2C and SPI have identical speed and pin requirements
I2C operates at gigahertz speeds
questions.explanation: I2C's two-wire advantage comes at the cost of lower bandwidth. The open-drain design and shared bus introduce capacitive loading and require arbitration, limiting speeds to kHz range. SPI trades additional pins (CS lines, no clock on shared line) for much higher MHz speeds with no arbitration overhead.

questions.q: In SPI communication, what is the role of the Chip Select (CS) signal?
options: It provides the clock signal for data transfer
It selects which slave device will communicate with the master while others remain inactive
It controls the data transfer direction
It sets the baud rate for communication
questions.explanation: CS (often active-low) is asserted by the master to select a specific slave device before transmission. Only the selected slave responds to clock and data lines; others ignore them. This allows multiple slaves on the same SPI bus without addressing overhead, unlike I2C.

questions.q: Why is CAN bus communication considered more reliable than simple UART for distributed embedded systems?
options: CAN bus operates at higher speeds
CAN bus includes message arbitration, error detection (CRC), and can retransmit corrupted messages, while UART has no error checking
UART is actually more reliable than CAN bus
Reliability depends only on the wires used, not the protocol
questions.explanation: CAN implements CSMA/CA arbitration (preventing collisions on multi-master systems), CRC error detection, and automatic retransmission of damaged frames. UART has no built-in error detection or recovery. This robustness makes CAN ideal for safety-critical automotive and industrial environments.

questions.q: Which protocol would be most appropriate for real-time sensor data collection from 15 distributed sensors in an industrial environment?
options: UART (one connection per sensor)
SPI (with many CS lines to different sensors)
I2C (multiple sensors on a shared low-speed bus might have timing issues)
CAN bus (multi-device capability, error handling, and real-time characteristics)
questions.explanation: CAN bus excels at distributed sensor networks with multiple devices on a single cable pair, built-in error recovery, and real-time message prioritization. With 15 devices, UART and SPI become impractical (wiring complexity). While I2C could work, CAN's robustness and error handling make it the industry standard for such applications.
